---
title: "CLI wiring: from a parsed netlist to a solved circuit"
subtitle: "T6 · Phase T6.2 · `va-cli`"
---

::: {.callout-note}
**Status: code complete.** 16 tests, all green. Golden generation itself lives in `xtask`, not
here (`t6-integration/03-validation.qmd`) — this crate's job stops at solving the circuit and
printing/plotting the answer.
:::

## Goal

Turn a parsed `Netlist` plus an optional compiled `.va` model into real `ModelInstance`s, solve
whichever analysis was requested, and report the answer — the one binary every ladder-rung demo
in this project actually runs.

## Where it fits

```
 Netlist + Vec<Module> ──[build_instance per device]──► Vec<Box<dyn ModelInstance>>
                                    │
                     va_core::dc::{operating_point, sweep} / va_transient::integrator::{run, run_dynamic}
```

`va_cli::load` is the shared prelude (parse the netlist, optionally compile a `.va` model) both
`run_sim` (the binary's own entry point) and `va-harness` (comparing against golden) call —
added specifically so the harness gets real `OperatingPoint`/`Waveform` values back, not
`run_sim`'s printed stdout re-parsed.

## The idea

**A device resolves to an instance by name, preferring a compiled Verilog-A model over a
built-in primitive.** `build_instance` first checks whether any compiled module's name matches
the device's own `model` string (`resistor` devices match `resistor.va`, if one was compiled);
if none matches, `reference_instance` falls back to `va-abi`'s hand-written primitives
(`resistor`/`capacitor`/`diode`/`bjt`). This is why the same `circuits/mos_dc.net` deck can be
solved either via `--model models/mosfet.va` (through the real frontend→codegen pipeline) or,
for a device like `bjt` with no `.va` counterpart, purely through the hand-written reference
model — the netlist file itself doesn't need to know which.

**A voltage source's own value overrides a compiled model's first parameter, not the other way
around** — the SPICE convention that an `R`/`C` line's own value sets the model's primary
parameter (`build_from_model`), so `R1 in out 1000` against a compiled `resistor.va` still means
1 kΩ, not whatever `resistor.va`'s own declared default happens to be.

**`solve_transient` always seeds `x0 = vec![0.0; dim]`** — no `.ic`/`UIC` support (this crate's
own stated limitation, threaded through `t3-core/02-newton.qmd`, `t4-transient/01-integration.qmd`,
and `t6-integration/03-validation.qmd`'s QSPICE-comparison fixes). For a deck with no
time-varying source, this is the plain fixed-instance path (`va_transient::integrator::run`); a
`SIN`-sourced deck instead rebuilds that one source fresh every step
(`va_transient::integrator::run_dynamic`), since `ModelInstance::load` has no time parameter to
read a clock from.

## The code

```rust
pub fn load(netlist: &str, model: Option<&str>) -> Result<(Netlist, Vec<Module>)>;

pub fn solve_dc(net: &Netlist, compiled: &[Module]) -> Result<va_core::dc::OperatingPoint>;
pub fn solve_dc_sweep(net: &Netlist, compiled: &[Module], sweep: &DcSweep)
    -> Result<Vec<(f64, OperatingPoint)>>;
pub fn solve_transient(net: &Netlist, compiled: &[Module]) -> Result<Waveform>;

pub fn run_sim(netlist: &str, model: Option<&str>, analysis: Analysis, plot: Option<&str>)
    -> Result<()>;
```

`reference_instance`'s dispatch table, the seam every new built-in primitive joins:

```rust
match dev.model.as_str() {
    "resistor" => Box::new(Resistor::new(p, n, value()?)),
    "capacitor" => Box::new(Capacitor::new(p, n, value()?)),
    "diode" => Box::new(Diode::new(p, n, 1e-14, 1.0, VT_NOMINAL)),
    "bjt" => Box::new(Bjt::new(b, c, e, 1e-15, 100.0, 1.0, VT_NOMINAL)),  // added 2026-07-18
    other => bail!("device `{}` references unknown model `{other}` ...", dev.name),
}
```

## It works

```console
$ cargo test -p va-cli --lib
running 16 tests
test tests::divider_solves_through_codegen_pipeline ... ok
test tests::mos_dc_solves_through_codegen_pipeline ... ok
test tests::diode_iv_sweep_solves_through_codegen_pipeline ... ok
test tests::rc_step_solves_through_the_real_pipeline ... ok
test tests::rectifier_solves_through_the_real_pipeline ... ok
test tests::ring_osc_sustains_oscillation_through_the_real_pipeline ... ok
test tests::hierarchical_divider_solves_through_codegen_pipeline ... ok
...
test result: ok. 16 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured; 0 filtered out; finished in 4.17s
```

Every one of these drives a real, committed `.net` file (and, where relevant, a real `.va`
model) through the actual pipeline end to end — not a hand-built instance list — and checks the
answer against a closed-form/analytic value, the same discipline every crate in this project
follows.

## Limitations

Same cold-start limitation `t3-core/02-newton.qmd`/`t4-transient/01-integration.qmd` note: no
`.ic`/`UIC` support, so `solve_transient` can't reproduce a circuit whose demo genuinely depends
on starting from a specific non-zero operating point (the ring oscillator's hand-built fixture
does; the same topology run through this crate's own cold start turned out to reproduce the
oscillation anyway, but only confirmed empirically, not guaranteed by design —
`t4-transient/03-events.qmd`). `--plot` only supports transient runs (a DC operating point is a
single point, not a waveform).

## What's next

`t6-integration/03-validation.qmd` — comparing what this crate solves against real QSPICE
output.
